speaker: interviewer
text: It's August the 8th, 1995. The survivor is Ernest Braunstein. Norman Pell is interviewer, Los Angeles, California-- excuse me, Beverly Hills, USA. It will be in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Norman Pell. We are in Beverly Hills, California. Today is August 8, 1995. And for the Survivors of the Shoah Foundation, we are interviewing Mr. Ernest Braunstein. The interview will be in English. Good morning Mr. Braunstein. Would you give us your full name and let us know where you were born and when?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Ernest Braunstein. I was born in Germany [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on February 12th, 1924. When I was a young boy, like one-year-old, we moved to Romania and my father-- actually in Romania, which was changed back to Hungary, Romania in a matter of 15 years or so. But I'd been raised mostly in Romanian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my family, my father was in the wholesale fruit business.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was Aladar Braunstein. My mother's name was Aranka Braunstein, which her maiden name was [? Farency ?].

speaker: interviewer
text: And where in Romania did you move to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Called in Romanian called Oradea Mare, in Hungarian is Nagyvarad. And a little from there was 24 kilometers was a little town which called Mezotelegd. That's what-- where I was raised by and went to school, in Oradea Mare.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell us about your childhood. What was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: How big was the town? What was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in the town was a small town when I was raised. But Nagyvarad was a big town already. And I went to school there, and both in Hungarian and then in Romanian when it was changed. I had a very good young life, you know. My father was in the business, you know. I remember that, you know, I get a bicycle on mine 13th birthday, so I become a rich person. Everybody was look at me that I had a bicycle.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I could say that we was in-- in that time, you know, it was three categories, you know, poor people, middle people, and rich people. Well, I consider we was in the middle class people, you know, at that time. Did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had a brother which I never remembered, you know, passed away before I was born. Then but I have a brother about three years younger than I am, which is still alive. He lives here in Palm Spring.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell us about the town. Was it mostly a Jewish town?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very conservative far as Jews concerned. Was a lot of Jews there. And I could say that I remember in my young life, the Jews was very well treated at that time. In Romania, the Jews had right to do a lot of thing. The Jews, most of them, the reason was anti-Semitism, but the Jews had the money. The Jews had the power. So it was a very well, nice time at that time where I was raised.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell us about the schools that you attended.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went to cheder, you know, first, you know. I had a [INAUDIBLE] 'til I was about 13-year-old. We was an religious family I came from. You know, my both grandfather had a big beard and were religious. And when I went to grammar school and then I went to high school and 1940s, you know, I went from Romania to Hungary, in Budapest, to my uncle, where I was-- went to the first year in college in Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- in the town, what were the major industries in the town where-- what did people do for a living, most of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well most people was, if you talk about Jews, they was most of them in the business. They owned the businesses. They was in any kind of business you can imagine. They owned houses. They owned properties. So they was very well situated far as the Jews concerned in that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you recall going on vacations? What was-- what was life like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Every year we went difference places, you know. My young life, you know, was very well situated there, you know, with my brother, you know. We went difference places, you know, every summer. My father was, in-- like I said, in pretty well good shape. He was making money. And my mother was a housewife, you know. She was, like I remember, my mother that my father, Friday night, came home with two chicken. But when we came to the table, you know, two chicken supposed to have four legs. But we had only two legs at the table. My mother give the other two legs, you know, to the neighbors, you know, they was poorer them. They needed it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a religious home, your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We had kosher home at home, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And observed the holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we did. Definitely, you know, we observed every holiday. And my father was, at that time, you know, very religious, you know. My grandfather, like I said, was very religious, you know. We called it the Talmud [NON-ENGLISH]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You indicate that life in Romania was very nice and enjoyable. As you grew up, did you experience any anti-Semitism at all? Were-- could you see--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not as much than after the war. Before the war, no, I didn't. I'll be honest with you, it was. I mean, occasionally, yes, it was. But not as much, you know, than after the war or during the war. But it was, but not as much as that we heard in any other countries, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you grew up and then you become a young man. And then what happens?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in-- like I said, in 1940, I went up to Hungary, Budapest, you know, where I was working for my uncle. He has a chemistry factory, you know, which we-- he didn't had any child and he took me there, you know. And I started the first year college over there. And I remember that day, you know, when I came home from the school, you know, the Hungarian took me aside and let-- put my pants down, you know, to find out that I'm a Jew or not. The other time when they put me in a labor camp and they give me 24 hours to go home and get back to the camp, you know. And at that time, in 1943, they shipped me to Yugoslavia. We call Bor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we get to that, tell us in a little more detail, you're working there in-- in Hungary.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then all of a sudden--

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked for my uncle, you know, It was not as a working [INAUDIBLE], but I was studying, and he wanted me to teach me, you know, the business.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then all of a sudden, one day out of the blue--

speaker: interviewee
text: Out of the blue, you know, they-- they just got me on a street, you know, and took me away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they doing this to other Jewish people at the time as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they did. Yes, they did, you know. That was the-- the Hungarian who was collaborated with the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when in Hungary did you first feel that there was a real problem?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we felt it. But I would say in 1942 they started a little movement over there, you know. And we have to go with the yellow star, you know they put us on ourselves. So we started to get problems, you know. But in 1943, was already much worst.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they-- this gang of Hungarian youth get a hold of you, and you're told to report somewhere?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Not youth, you know, I'm talking about Hungarian soldiers, you know. And they put me, like I said, in a camp, you know. And they give me 24 hours to go to pack, you know, and report back. And about a week later we were shipped, you know, to-- like I said, to Yugoslavia, where they call it Bor, for a labor camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you-- how did you make the trip? By train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They put us on a train. And then we went to a small train with two cars between the hills, you know, where, as you know, in Yugoslavia, it's a lot of hills. And then we started to work over there, you know. They put us to build train-- how they call that?-- for the train to go--

speaker: interviewer
text: The tracks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tracks. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in this labor camp-- first of all, on the train, how many-- how many people were on the train that-- that took you to the labor camp? Was it a big train, a lot of people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were a lot of people. It was difference camps there, you know. We was on the top of the hill, you know. If I remember, we had over there about four or five difference camps, they had people there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many people were-- totally were in the labor camp in Bor?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say around 15,000. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They took about 15,000 people over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell us a little about your life in the labor camp in Bor.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, let me tell you one of the incidents what happened. First of all, we worked from morning 'til night. And one occasion, one of my friend, which I don't think he did anything, but they went and they put him on the side. And they hanged him by putting his hand in the back. And with his own belts, they hanged him up, you know, 'til he [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] couldn't do anything. And I could tell you that, that I went there and I was giving him water, you know. And at the same time, one of the Germans saw what I did, you know, and caught me. And he did the same thing to me. He hanged me 'til I get revived, you know, a few times with water.

speaker: interviewer
text: So finally, you know, they let me loose, you know. And I couldn't even pick up my arms. It was that bad. My friends was feeding me for a few days 'til I get my arm back. And what with the living quarters like in Bor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a barracks, you know. It was four-, five-story high barracks, you know, with little hay or mattress or even nothing, you know. That's the way we were sleeping there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were you fed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, feeding wasn't too bad, you know. We was getting three meals a day. And the reason why, we was working, so they felt, you know, if-- 'til we can work, you know, they was feeding us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell us what a normal work day was like.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we went out early in the morning, you know. And we was digging the hill, you know, with very hard work, I can tell you that much. You know, we was sweating, you know. But it was something that the Germans and the Hungarians both was there, was watching us, you know. And it was getting out of us everything what they could, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the workplace where you were doing the digging, how far was that from was from your barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: We was walking approximately two miles every morning and two miles back, you know, our camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your workday started what time and ended what time?

speaker: interviewee
text: It started, you know, approximately like 7:00 in the morning. And we worked till about 5:00, 6 o'clock at night.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there only men in the-- were there women there too?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was only men.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did-- was there any kind of a social life at all between you guys?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it wasn't. We have no time. And not only time, we had plenty time. They didn't allow us do anything, you know. We was very tired. We came home. We was glad to have something to eat, you know. And then we was just talking or trying to do something. But 7:00, 8o'clock, we went to bed, where we had to get up early in the morning to get back to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long do you figure you were in Bor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Approximately I would say I was there about 10 months or so.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, one day, they picks us up, you know, an d we started to walk back from Yugoslavia. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Germans and the Hungarians, you know, was-- was guarded. They come in horseback, you know. They was started and we was marching on feet, getting where we didn't know at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now again, this just happened one morning. You get up and they tell you to-- was there any indication? Was there any problems?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we find out that the Russians was pushing and starting to come through there, so they was taking us back, you know, whatever. We find out later on that we are headed to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- they start you walking?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many people are on this walk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, like I said, we started approximately around 15,000 people. And we had a lot of incidents that I could start to tell you what happened during the way. We was walking. And one of the pause, they give us 15, 20 minutes to rest, you know. And the Germans, they-- which was coming on horseback, they had something to do. So they put us out to lay down on the floor, you know. And they was find out how many people they-- that horse can jump, you know. That was the-- that was our 15, 20 minutes resting period. And finally we arrived. The name, Crvenka, which is-- was, if I remember right, a brick factory. And one morning they ask for volunteers, about 100 people. And I was one of them who volunteered to get out. And they put us in a side of a hill to dig a hole. And we didn't know what that really meant. But we dig the hole. And late afternoon, you know, we went back to the factory, which I said it was a brick factory. And we find out that-- But started to get dark. And they started to ask for volunteers again, 200, 300 people at a time. And we find out they took them to the hill site where we was digging up and they started to shoot them. They started to kill them. And that started to go on and on. And we heard the machine guns. And I happened to go up in a chimney where I find out what's happening. And I came down and I tell my friends, you know, what's going on. And at that time--

speaker: interviewer
text: So the ditch that you had dug, that the workers had dug, were used as a place for them to kill them?

speaker: interviewee
text: They tried to kill us, you know, so they don't know where-- probably they get an order not to bring us further up, so probably they give us an order to get rid of us. But they couldn't kill every one of them. I can tell you that-- that when we get walking to the next, we was maybe only a few thousand left already. And probably they tried not to, during the day, to see, you know, what they doing, so they did every killing during the night. And I had that much, that when we was walking back, we had one chance that we can do the people who cannot walk further, they can put-- get down on their knee, you know, and they shoot them right on the spot. And that was our safe regard, that if you can't walk, you have right to die.

speaker: interviewer
text: And many people were killed that way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Many people were that way, first of all. We couldn't had too much food. And, you know, especially older people, you know, they couldn't walk. I [INAUDIBLE] that march. We started in a very warm, summer days. And I could give you another incident, you know, when I was walking back, somebody threw away-- they couldn't carry any more-- a big leather jacket, which I picked it up. And I was carrying that through the summer 'til we get-- finally we started to get cold, you know. And I could tell you later on, that saved my life and two friends of my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the-- where did you-- during this march, where did you sleep? What kind of-- how were you fed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well-- well, was-- feeding was once in a while, like if they stopped us and it was from a truck what they was making food, you know. And they give us what they could. But wasn't enough. It wasn't enough food what they was giving at the time. And coming-- marching back, in camps when we stopped to sleep, it was sleeping right on the ground, you know, where hid us the dark. They'd stop. And that's what we slept.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned before that the German soldiers who were on horseback played this game where you would get down and they would take the horses and jump over the people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. You know, they took five and then they jumped over. They put another one, that's six still. The last one, finally they get jumped over. The horse couldn't jump it. They get killed there. That was our 20 minutes or so rest periods.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long do you figure this march took place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that march, like I said, we started in the summer, you know. And we run into the winter when finally we hit Germany. But I can tell you that much like before, that I get that leather jacket. And we don't have no more shoes, you know. We put papers, you know, to-- under our feet, you know, far as the start to winter, snowing. And I can tell you that much, that we put the snow, you know, to be higher for our-- to sleep, you know. So many, many people died right on the spot that day, just couldn't take it anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: During this march, were there any other places where you stopped and volunteered or had other jobs that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. After that, we stopped in a place called-- in a slaughter house. I remember that place, you know. We stopped in a slaughter house. And at that time, we was not eating already for about two days. And we was very hungry. We hardly had anything. And I went to a German officer and I-- that I happened to spoke German. And I told him, if you don't give us any food, then you better kill us here where we can't go further. And at that time, within two hours, I never forget, we was getting a goulash. And that was-- we filled our stomach up. And we went, started to walk again. And one incident happened. We stopped in another factory, where they was 10 volunteer. They asked for it. And then I was volunteered again, you know. And they took us to a ship to unload a ship, where I changed mine shoes. There was shoes, food, all kind of medicine. It was a filled-- the ship filled with a lot of clothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a cargo ship.

speaker: interviewee
text: A cargo ship, which we went there to help unload. And I can tell you that much, that I changed my shoes. I changed clothing. And I went and I told my other 10 people, you know, to-- we started to get a case of marmalade, a case of other kind of [INAUDIBLE], and we took that back to the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at the door they ask, you know, what's that? So I told them that the captain give it to us, which it was lie. We stole that-- most of that stuff. And that was about late afternoon when we went back to the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: And about two hours later, an officer came and started to look, you know, for the people who were bringing that stuff for the other people. They find out that the captain didn't give it to us, that we stoled it. And they started to say that if nobody is going to come out, they're going to shoot every other people if somebody won't come along and tell you what happened. And instead of letting a lot of people die, I came out and I told them that yes, I just thought, you know, that will be OK to bring it in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And two German officer came down, you know, took me down and blindfolded me that they want to shoot me. And at the time when they blindfolded, I heard the door open up in the camp. And must have been a high-ranking officer comes in and asks, what's going on here? I happened to, like I said, spoke German. I understand what he said. And he says-- one of them. He said that I was stealing, you know, food, bringing for the other people here. I said-- and the officer said, who give the order, you know, that you can kill him? Put him back. Lock him up. We'll find out what happened later with him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I never forget. It was a two-story building where they put me in. And they locked me in. And in the corner of the building was bombed in a corner. And it was about, I don't know, a 10-inch hole I saw there. And I happened to know that when we walked in, it was water near that building. And with my new shoes, you know, I knocked a little hole as big as that I could get out of that. And I jumped into the water, you know, much as I could. And a lot of ship was, at that time, bombed over there, you know, ruined ship. And I was there all night, 'til in the morning, you know, they was pushing again us. They was putting everybody close to the water. And I swim back again to mine people. And that's the way I saved my life at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: During the walk, you passed through cities. What was the feeling of the people who were living in these towns?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, let me explain. The Yugoslav people, they begged us to run away. They're hiding us. They put food, you know, along the highway, what they was risking their own life, you know, to-- some German would see that they're putting food there, you know, they would kill them too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did we get to the food? You know, we was five, six people. We'd talk together. And we run until we picked up some food and run back to the line, you know. And one or two get killed between. Excuse me.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the way you were treated, you know, along the way. And when we hit further to the Muslim section of Yugoslavia, we was treated again very bad, I can tell you that.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what respect?

speaker: interviewee
text: Respect that I wanted incident outright to hide. I thought, you know, I'm still in-- in Yugoslavia, which was supposed to be Yugoslavia. And I was running away with another friend of mine. And we hided-- you know, I don't know how they call that. You know, in Europe they was putting corn away for the winter. It's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Silo.

speaker: interviewee
text: Silo. And we was hiding there, you know, during the night. And it started to get light in the morning and the owner of the house came out and they saw us and started to beg us to run away from here, while they're going to call the-- the Germans, you know. And I-- I couldn't blame them, you know. The reason why they was-- you know, if they saw us over there, they probably would kill them, you know, so we had to get away from there. In the meantime, you know, we was trying to get away. And we find it that on a run in a little wooded hill, we saw, you know, our people marching. And we get back to our people again, you know. And we had no choice that we started to march again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the march continues through Yugoslavia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Then we hit Yugoslavia. We hit Romania. We went through Hungary, and all the way to the border Germany, to the border of Germany, which they putting over there, they put us as a cattle, they're putting into a cattle--

speaker: interviewer
text: Car.

speaker: interviewee
text: --car, you know. And that's the way they took us to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you recall how long the-- how long did it take from the time you got on the train 'til you arrived at Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would say it took us about four months, four, five months we was walking.

speaker: interviewer
text: The walk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then the train ride from-- when you got on the train, how long do you figure you were on the train, going to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I would say probably a day or so, you know, which we was cattles, you know. We have no restroom, no food, no nothing. So many people even died there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you arrive in Auschwitz. What happens then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they started to select people to the left, people to the right, you know. And they-- people to the left, you know, they took us through a line where we have to undress. And they took all the jewelry or anything valuable we had with us, you know, we have-- they took everything else. We was given some striped kind of clothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they took us from there to a camp, which, I don't know, also it was barracks. And I remember it was a winter day when we-- we went there. And they-- before we get into the barracks, it was a lot of benches there, you know. And they told us to sit down on it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We'll continue in a minute.
